key_insight: The paper studies how strictly proper multiclass losses can be re-shaped to change optimization geometry while still preserving target-distribution identification at the population level.
engineering_relevance: For classification systems, it suggests loss design can affect robustness, calibration, and training dynamics even when asymptotic correctness is maintained.
method: CAPM: a class-aware quadratic Bregman scoring rule with analyzed regret, curvature, range, and gradient bounds.
HPG: a strongly convex loss with constrained log-cosh ridge structure.
APMS: HPG plus an annealed probability-margin penalty, with exact bounds on penalty range and target displacement.
evidence_signal: Controlled five-seed experiments on Digits, Wisconsin breast cancer, and synthetic noisy/imbalanced settings show the candidates are often close to cross-entropy on clean data and sometimes better under noise, but the comparisons are presented as descriptive rather than significance evidence.
watch_item: Ablations did not show consistent gains from the proposed graph, ridge, or margin components, so the added complexity may not reliably improve performance.
paper_title: Structured Proper Loss Geometries for Multiclass Classification: Theory and Controlled Empirical Evaluation
